lists.title: Yoruba A1 Grammar
lists.description: Foundations: pronouns, tone, the copulas, location, negation, and the progressive.

lists.title: Yoruba A2 Grammar
lists.description: Aspect (perfect, future), objects, quality verbs, demonstratives, commands.

lists.title: Yoruba B1 Grammar
lists.description: Relatives, ni-focus, serial verbs, conditionals, comparison.

lists.title: Yoruba C2 Grammar
lists.description: Mastery: proverbs, the respect register, and the discourse particles that carry tone of voice.

points.title: Subject pronouns (mo, o, ó…)
points.function: Say who does what
points.explanation:
The short subject pronouns are mo (I), o (you), ó (he/she/it), a (we), ẹ (you all/polite), wọ́n (they). They sit right before the verb: Mo lọ (I went/go), Ó wá (He came).

Yoruba verbs never change form — the pronoun and small particles do all the work.
points.culture_note: Ẹ and wọ́n double as respectful singulars — using the plural for an elder is basic politeness.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

points.title: Tones change meaning
points.function: Hear and write the three tones
points.explanation:
Yoruba has three tones: high (á), mid (a, unmarked), low (à) — and they distinguish words: ọkọ́ (hoe), ọkọ (husband), ọkọ̀ (vehicle).

Every syllable carries a tone; writing the marks is part of spelling, not decoration.
points.culture_note: Talking drums imitate tone patterns — a drummer can 'speak' a proverb because tone carries that much meaning.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.sentence: Fọ {{answer}} rẹ kí o tó jẹun.
drills.translation: Wash your hands before you eat.
drills.hint: hand — with subdot and high tone

drills.translation: I bought a new vehicle yesterday.

points.title: The copulas ni and jẹ́
points.function: Say what something is
points.explanation:
Identity uses ni after the noun phrase: Olùkọ́ ni mí (I am a teacher — lit. teacher is me), Ta ni é? (Who are you?). Jẹ́ states category or profession before the noun: Mo jẹ́ olùkọ́ (I am a teacher).

Both are A1 essentials; ni also powers focus later.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.hint: the copula for professions and qualities

drills.translation: I am a teacher (it is a teacher that I am).

drills.hint: the copula for professions and qualities
the copula for professions and qualities

points.title: Location with wà and ní
points.function: Say where people and things are
points.explanation: 'Be located' is wà, and places take the preposition ní (sí for motion): Ó wà ní ilé (He is at home), Mo wà ní ọjà (I am at the market). With motion verbs: Mo lọ sí ilé-ìwé (I went to school).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: The book is on the table.
The child is at the market.

points.title: Negation with kò
points.function: Say what is not so
points.explanation: Kò before the verb negates it: Kò lọ (He didn't go), Mi ò fẹ́ (I don't want — mo becomes mi and kò often shortens to ò after it). Negating wà uses sí: Kò sí ni ilé (He is not at home); 'there isn't' is kò sí.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: There is no food at home.

points.title: Progressive ń
points.function: Say what is happening right now
points.explanation: The preverb ń (high tone, written with its own letter) makes the progressive: Mo ń lọ (I am going), Ó ń jẹun (She is eating), Wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́ (They are working).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

points.title: Plural with àwọn
points.function: Talk about more than one
points.explanation:
Nouns don't change for number; put àwọn before the noun when plurality matters: ọmọ (child/children) → àwọn ọmọ (the children), àwọn olùkọ́ (the teachers).

Numbers make àwọn unnecessary: ọmọ méjì (two children).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: I saw the children at the market.

points.title: Questions with ṣé and kí ni
points.function: Ask yes/no and 'what' questions
points.explanation: Ṣé in front of a statement makes a yes/no question: Ṣé o fẹ́ omi? (Do you want water?). Content questions use kí ni (what), ta ni (who), níbo (where): Kí ni orúkọ rẹ? (What is your name?), Níbo lo ń lọ? (Where are you going?).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.hint: the copula that follows a question word

points.title: Possession (mi, rẹ, rẹ̀ / ti)
points.function: Say whose something is
points.explanation:
Possessive pronouns follow the noun: ìwé mi (my book), ilé rẹ (your house), ọkọ̀ rẹ̀ (his/her vehicle), ilé wa (our house).

Between two nouns, possession is just order (or ti): ilé bàbá (father's house).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

points.title: Perfect ti
points.function: Say what has already happened
points.explanation:
Ti before the verb marks completion: Mo ti jẹun (I have eaten), Ó ti lọ (She has already left), Wọ́n ti dé (They have arrived).

With kò it means 'not yet': Kò tíì dé (He hasn't arrived yet).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: Father has arrived from the farm.

points.title: Future yóò / máa
points.function: Say what will happen
points.explanation:
Two future markers: yóò (with full pronouns: Ó yóò lọ → usually yó lọ) and máa, the everyday one: Mo máa lọ (I will go), A máa rìn (We will walk).

Negative future uses kò ní í: Kò ní í lọ (He won't go).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: They will build a new house.
I will buy a new vehicle.

points.title: Object pronouns
points.function: Say who receives the action
points.explanation:
Object pronouns follow the verb and copy its final vowel's tone pattern: rí mi (see me), rí ẹ (see you), rí i (see him/her/it — vowel echo), rí wa (see us), rí yín (see you all), rí wọn (see them).

Mo rí i (I saw him).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: I saw him/her at the market.
They saw us at the market.

points.title: Adjectives are verbs (dára, tóbi)
points.function: Describe qualities the Yoruba way
points.explanation:
Most quality words are stative VERBS: Ó dára (It is good), Ilé tóbi (The house is big), Ó gùn (It is long) — no copula needed.

Before a noun they become participles or use relative tí: ilé ńlá (a big house), ilé tí ó dára (a house that is good).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.hint: quality word used as a verb
quality word used as a verb

points.title: Demonstratives yìí and yẹn
points.function: Point out this one and that one
points.explanation:
Yìí (this) and yẹn (that) follow the noun: ìwé yìí (this book), ọkùnrin yẹn (that man). Plural combines with àwọn: àwọn ìwé yìí (these books).

Náà after a noun means 'the (aforementioned)': ilé náà (the house in question).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

points.title: Imperatives and má (don't)
points.function: Tell people to do and not to do
points.explanation:
Commands are the bare verb: Wá! (Come!), Jókòó! (Sit down!). Politeness adds jọ̀wọ́ (please) or the plural ẹ: Ẹ jọ̀wọ́, ẹ wá (Please come).

The negative command is má (or má ṣe): Má lọ! (Don't go!).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

points.title: Relative clauses with tí
points.function: Describe things with 'who/that/which'
points.explanation:
Tí introduces relative clauses: ọkùnrin tí ó wá (the man who came), ìwé tí mo rà (the book that I bought), oúnjẹ tí wọ́n sè (the food they cooked).

Inside the clause the normal pronouns stay.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: The man who came is a teacher.
The book that I bought is good.

drills.sentence: Obìnrin {{answer}} ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ níbí dára.
drills.translation: The woman who works here is good.
The food that you cooked is delicious.
The house that they built is big.
The child who was sleeping has woken up.

points.title: Focus with ni
points.function: Emphasize one part of the sentence
points.explanation:
Moving a phrase to the front and adding ni focuses it: Adé ni ó lọ (It was Adé who went), Ọjà ni mo lọ (It's the market I went to), Àná ni wọ́n dé (It was yesterday they arrived).

Question words use the same frame: Ta ni ó wá? (Who came?).
points.culture_note: This ni-focus is everywhere in real speech — answers to questions almost always use it.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.hint: focus marker after the fronted word

drills.translation: It is the market that I went to.
It was I who cooked the food.
It was yesterday that they arrived.
It was a book that I bought.
It is here that we live.

points.title: Serial verbs
points.function: Chain verbs to express one complex action
points.explanation:
Yoruba stacks verbs without 'and': mú ìwé wá (take book come = bring the book), gbé e lọ (carry it go = take it away), ra oúnjẹ fún mi (buy food give me = buy food for me).

Each verb keeps its own meaning; together they map one event.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: I took money to the market.

points.title: Conditionals with bí … bá
points.function: Make 'if' sentences
points.explanation:
Real conditions use bí (or tí) … bá: Bí o bá kàwé, wàá yege (If you study, you will pass), Bí òjò bá rọ̀, a ó dúró ní ilé (If it rains, we will stay home).

Bá sits right before the verb of the if-clause.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: If you study, you will pass.
drills.hint: the second half of the frame

drills.sentence: Bí òjò {{answer}} rọ̀, a ó dúró.
drills.translation: If it rains, we will stay.
drills.hint: the second half of the frame

drills.sentence: Bí a {{answer}} ṣiṣẹ́, a ó rí owó.
drills.translation: If we work, we will get money.
drills.hint: the second half of the frame
the first half of the frame

drills.sentence: Bí ó bá {{answer}}, a ó lọ.
drills.translation: If he arrives, we will go.
If they are asleep, don't make noise.
drills.hint: the second half of the frame

points.title: Comparatives with ju … lọ
points.function: Compare people and things
points.explanation: Comparison wraps ju … lọ around the standard: Ó ga ju mí lọ (He is taller than me), Ilé yìí tóbi ju ilé yẹn lọ (This house is bigger than that one). The quality word itself never changes.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.sentence: Ilé yìí tóbi ju ilé yẹn {{answer}}.
drills.translation: This house is bigger than that house.
drills.hint: the closing word of the comparison frame
the closing word of the comparison frame

drills.translation: This food is tastier than ours.
I like tea more than coffee.

drills.sentence: Ọkọ̀ yìí yára ju ti mi {{answer}}.
drills.translation: This vehicle is faster than mine.
drills.hint: the closing word of the comparison frame

points.title: Habitual máa ń
points.function: Talk about routines
points.explanation: Máa ń (often written maa n) marks habits: Mo máa ń jí ní aago mẹ́fà (I usually wake at six), Wọ́n máa ń lọ sí ṣọ́ọ̀ṣì (They usually go to church). Negative habitual is kì í: Kì í mu ọtí (He doesn't (habitually) drink).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.sentence: Mo {{answer}} ń jí ní aago mẹ́fà.
drills.translation: I usually wake up at six o'clock.

drills.sentence: Wọ́n {{answer}} ń ṣiṣẹ́ ní ọjọ́ Sátidé.
A máa {{answer}} jẹun ní aago méje.
drills.translation: We usually eat at seven o'clock.
He doesn't usually wake up early.

drills.sentence: Màmá {{answer}} ń sè oúnjẹ ní alẹ́.
drills.translation: Mother usually cooks in the evening.

points.title: Reported speech with pé
points.function: Report what people said and think
points.explanation: Pé introduces reported clauses after saying/thinking verbs: Ó sọ pé òun máa wá (He said that he would come), Mo mọ̀ pé o ti dé (I know that you have arrived). Note òun — the special third-person pronoun used inside reports to refer back to the speaker.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: He said that he would come.
I know that you have arrived.
He thought that I had gone.
They believe that it will rain.
I heard that you were ill.

drills.sentence: Ó sọ fún mi {{answer}} kí n dúró.
drills.translation: He told me that I should wait.

points.title: Impersonal wọ́n (passive meaning)
points.function: Say what 'is done' without a doer
points.explanation: Yoruba has no passive; impersonal wọ́n (they) covers it: Wọ́n ti kọ́ ilé náà (The house has been built — lit. they have built it), Wọ́n ń pè ọ́ (You are being called). Context, not form, signals the passive reading.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: The house has been built (lit. they have built it).
You are being called (lit. they are calling you).
A new school will be opened.
A new road is being built.

points.title: Although: bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé
points.function: Concede a point and continue
points.explanation:
The concessive frame bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé … (even though …) sets up contrast: Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé òjò rọ̀, a lọ (Even though it rained, we went).

A shorter alternative is síbẹ̀ (nevertheless) linking two clauses.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.sentence: Bí ó {{answer}} jẹ́ pé òjò rọ̀, a lọ.
drills.translation: Even though it rained, we went.

drills.sentence: Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ {{answer}} ó rẹ̀ mí, mo parí iṣẹ́.
drills.translation: Although I was tired, I finished the work.
drills.hint: the closing word of the frame

drills.sentence: Bí ó {{answer}} jẹ́ pé kò sí owó, a rí oúnjẹ.
drills.translation: Although there was no money, we found food.

drills.sentence: Ó ṣàìsàn; {{answer}}, ó lọ síbi iṣẹ́.
drills.translation: He was ill; nevertheless, he went to work.

drills.sentence: Bí ó tilẹ̀ {{answer}} pé ilé jìnnà, a rìn.
drills.translation: Although the house is far, we walked.

points.title: Ní vs sí — at a place vs towards it
points.function: Say where something is vs where it's heading
points.explanation: Ní marks a static location (ó wà ní ilé — he is at home); sí marks direction or a goal (ó lọ sí ọjà — he went to the market). Verbs of rest take ní, verbs of motion take sí — mixing them is the classic learner giveaway.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: We are going to the market.
The book is on the table.

points.title: Question words (ta ni, níbo, báwo, èló)
points.function: Ask about people, places, manner, price, time, and reasons
points.explanation: Ta ni (who), níbo (where), báwo (how), èló (how much), nígbà wo (when), èéṣe/kí nìdí (why) — most pair with the focus ni you already know: Níbo ni o ń lọ? (where are you going?).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

points.title: Modals fẹ́, lè, gbọ́dọ̀ (want / can / must)
points.function: Add want-to, can, and must to any verb
points.explanation:
Fẹ́, lè, and gbọ́dọ̀ sit between the subject and the verb: mo fẹ́ jẹun (I want to eat), ó lè wẹ̀ (she can swim), o gbọ́dọ̀ lọ (you must go).

Negation wraps around them: kò lè wá (he can't come).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.sentence: O {{answer}} lọ sí ilé ìwé lónìí.
drills.translation: You must go to school today.

points.title: Láti + verb (to / in order to)
points.function: Chain a purpose or an infinitive onto a verb
points.explanation: Láti turns a verb into 'to do X': mo wá láti kọ́ èdè Yorùbá (I came to learn Yoruba); ó ṣòro láti jí (it's hard to wake up). After fẹ́ it's optional — both mo fẹ́ lọ and mo fẹ́ láti lọ occur.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: He wants to buy a new house.
It is hard to wake up early.
It is not easy to write a song.
We went out to buy food.

points.title: Kí clauses — wishes and indirect commands
points.function: Wish, bless, and tell people what should happen
points.explanation:
Kí opens a clause about what SHOULD happen: mo fẹ́ kí o wá (I want you to come), bàbá ní kí wọ́n jẹun (father said they should eat), kí ọjọ́ rẹ ó dára (may your day be good).

It's the engine of Yoruba blessings.
points.culture_note: Yoruba life runs on kí-blessings: kí ọjọ́ ó dára, kí ọlọ́run ó ṣọ́ ẹ (may God guard you) — expect them at every parting.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.sentence: Mo fẹ́ {{answer}} o wá ní àárọ̀.
drills.translation: I want you to come in the morning.
He said that we should wait.

drills.hint: to eat — bare verb inside the kí clause

drills.translation: I pray that he finds work.

drills.sentence: Sọ fún un {{answer}} ó pè mí.

points.title: Numbers after the noun (ìwé méjì, mélòó)
points.function: Count things and ask how many
points.explanation:
Numerals follow the noun: ìwé kan (one book), ọmọ méjì (two children), ọjọ́ méje (seven days).

Mélòó asks 'how many' from the same slot: ìwé mélòó? Standalone counting uses the ọ̀kan, èjì, ẹ̀ta series instead.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.sentence: Ọmọ {{answer}} ni ó wà ní ilé?
drills.translation: How many children are at home?
We saw three birds on the tree.
He lived there for five years.

points.title: Making nouns: olù-, oní-/alá-, ì-
points.function: Build people-words and idea-words from verbs and nouns
points.explanation: Olù- makes agents (olùkọ́ teacher ← kọ́ teach); oní- makes owners/sellers and assimilates hard (oní + ata → aláta pepper seller, oní + aṣọ → aláṣọ cloth seller); ì- makes action nouns (ìdùnnú joy ← dùn, ìbéèrè question ← béèrè); a- makes doers (akọrin singer).

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.sentence: {{answer}} wa kọ́ wa ní èdè Gẹ̀ẹ́sì.

drills.translation: I bought pepper from the pepper seller.
The cloth seller sells new cloth.
I am happy; my joy is great.
The singer has a good voice.

points.title: Serial verbs with fi and fún
points.function: Say with-what and for-whom inside the verb chain
points.explanation: Fi (take) smuggles an instrument into the chain: ó fi ọ̀bẹ gé ẹran (he took-knife cut meat = cut it with a knife). Fún (give) marks the beneficiary: ra ìwé fún mi (buy a book for me); fi X fún Y hands X over to Y.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: He cut the meat with a knife.
I tied a headscarf on my head.
They received the news with joy.
Do this work for your father.

points.title: Time clauses: nígbà tí, lẹ́yìn tí, kí … tó
points.function: Anchor events before, after, and when
points.explanation: Nígbà tí opens 'when'-clauses (nígbà tí mo dé — when I arrived), lẹ́yìn tí 'after', and kí … tó 'before' (kí o tó lọ — before you go). All lean on the tí you know from relative clauses.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.sentence: {{answer}} tí mo dé, òjò ń rọ̀.
drills.translation: When I arrived, it was raining.

drills.sentence: Nígbà {{answer}} ó jí, oòrùn ti yọ.
drills.translation: When he woke, the sun had already risen.

drills.sentence: A jẹun {{answer}} tí a parí iṣẹ́.
drills.translation: We ate after we finished the work.
Before you go, turn off the light.

drills.sentence: Ó sùn {{answer}} tí ó jẹun tán.
drills.translation: He slept after he finished eating.

drills.sentence: Kí ilẹ̀ {{answer}} ṣú, ẹ padà sílé.
drills.translation: Before it gets dark, come back home.

points.title: Chaining clauses with sì (and then)
points.function: String actions into a flowing narrative
points.explanation: Sì links same-subject clauses, sitting AFTER the subject of the second clause: ó dé ilé, ó sì jókòó (he got home and sat down). In commands it rides inside the kí clause: jókòó, kí o sì gbọ́.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: He arrived home and sat down.

drills.sentence: Mo ra ìwé, mo {{answer}} kà á.
drills.translation: I bought a book and read it.
They ate and went to sleep.
She opened the door and entered.
Sit down and listen to me.

points.title: Reduplication: jíjẹ, kíkà, kíákíá
points.function: Turn verbs into gerunds and dial up adverbs
points.explanation:
Doubling the verb's first consonant + í makes a gerund: jẹ eat → jíjẹ eating, kà read → kíkà reading, rà buy → rírà buying.

Whole-word doubling intensifies adverbs: kíákíá quickly, díẹ̀díẹ̀ little by little, pẹ̀lẹ́pẹ̀lẹ́ gently.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: Reading books is good for children.
Money comes in little by little.
Buying a house costs a lot of money.

points.title: Ideophonic intensifiers (láúláú, roro)
points.function: Intensify like a native — each word has its own superlative
points.explanation:
Yoruba doesn't say 'very white'; each quality owns a dedicated ideophone: funfun láúláú (spotlessly white), dúdú kirikiri (jet black), pupa roro (glowing red), tutù nini (ice-cold), kún fọ́fọ́ (full to the brim), gbóná janjan (piping hot).

The pairs are fixed — swapping them sounds absurd.
points.culture_note: Ideophones are where Yoruba paints: the sound IS the meaning. Regional spellings vary — a native reviewer should confirm local forms.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: The bride's outfit is a glowing red.
The bag is full to the brim.

points.title: How-clauses with bí … ṣe
points.function: Talk about the way something happens
points.explanation: Bí + subject + ṣe + verb = 'how/the way that': bí mo ṣe rí i (how I saw it), ṣàlàyé bí ẹ̀rọ náà ṣe ń ṣiṣẹ́ (explain how the machine works). Mind the tones: ṣe (do) is the frame; sè (cook) just sounds close.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.sentence: Èyí ni bí mo {{answer}} rí i.
drills.translation: This is how I saw it.
drills.hint: the manner-frame verb after the subject

drills.sentence: Èyí ni {{answer}} mo ṣe rí i.
drills.translation: This is how I saw it.

drills.sentence: Ṣàlàyé bí ẹ̀rọ náà {{answer}} ń ṣiṣẹ́.
drills.hint: the manner-frame verb after the subject

drills.sentence: Mo fẹ́ mọ bí o ṣe {{answer}} oúnjẹ yìí.
drills.translation: I want to know how you cooked this food.
drills.hint: to cook — low tone, not the frame verb

drills.sentence: Wo bí ọmọ náà {{answer}} ń sáré!
drills.hint: the manner-frame verb after the subject

drills.sentence: Mo gbàgbé bí wọ́n {{answer}} pè é.
drills.translation: I forget how they pronounce it.
drills.hint: the manner-frame verb after the subject

points.title: Òwe — proverbs, the horses of speech
points.function: Cap an argument the way elders do
points.explanation: Òwe ni ẹṣin ọ̀rọ̀ — proverbs are the horses of speech: when talk gets lost, a proverb carries it home. They compress the grammar you know (habituals, focus, parallel clauses) into fixed wisdom, and quoting the right one grants instant authority.
points.culture_note: Introducing one with 'àwọn àgbà á máa sọ pé…' (the elders say…) is the polite frame for quoting òwe.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: Proverbs are the horses of speech.

drills.sentence: Bí ọmọdé bá ṣubú, á wo iwájú; bí àgbà bá ṣubú, á wo {{answer}}.
drills.translation: When a child falls he looks ahead; when an elder falls he looks back.

drills.sentence: Ọwọ́ ọmọdé kò tó pẹpẹ, ti àgbàlagbà kò wọ {{answer}}.
drills.translation: A child's hand can't reach the shelf; an elder's can't enter the gourd.

drills.sentence: Ẹnu àgbà ni obì ti ń {{answer}}.
drills.translation: It is in the elder's mouth that the kola nut ripens.
drills.hint: to ripen — wisdom matures with age

drills.sentence: Ọjọ́ gbogbo ni ti olè, ọjọ́ kan ni ti {{answer}}.
drills.translation: Every day belongs to the thief, one day belongs to the owner.
drills.hint: the owner (of the thing) — oní- word

drills.sentence: Àgbà kì í wà lọ́jà kí orí ọmọ tuntun {{answer}}.
drills.translation: An elder cannot be in the market and let the newborn's head hang askew.

points.title: Respect register: ẹ and ẹ̀yin
points.function: Address elders the way Yoruba demands
points.explanation:
Elders and superiors take the plural: the pronoun ẹ̀yin/ẹ replaces o even for ONE person — Ẹ káàárọ̀ (good morning, ma/sir), Ẹ jọ̀wọ́ (please), Ẹ ṣeun (thank you).

Greetings are an art: Ẹ kú iṣẹ́ salutes someone at work, Ẹ káàbọ̀ welcomes an arrival.
points.culture_note: Using bare 'o' with an elder is not casual, it's insolent. Greeting protocol (kú + situation: ẹ kú ìjókòó, ẹ kú àtijọ́…) is a register of its own.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.sentence: 'Ẹ {{answer}}, mà!' ọmọ náà kí ìyá àgbà.
drills.translation: 'Good morning, ma!' the child greeted the old woman.
Well done with your work, father.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} ni yóò kọ́ wa lẹ́kọ̀ọ́ lónìí.
drills.translation: YOU (respected one) will teach us today.

points.title: Discourse particles (o, ná, ṣebí, gan-an)
points.function: Add the warmth and edge writing can't show
points.explanation: Sentence-final o adds warmth or urgency (ó dàbọ̀ o!); ná means 'first / for now' (dúró ná); ṣebí opens a 'surely…?' check; gan-an intensifies ('really'); tilẹ̀ adds 'even'. These particles carry the tone of voice — leave them out and you sound like a textbook.

references.title: Live Lingua — free Yoruba courses (FSI/Peace Corps)

drills.translation: He didn't come; he didn't even send word.
